---
title: "Coding Mini-Project"
subtitle: "Huffman Encoder"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.85in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{tikz}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time estimate} & \~{}1.5 hours \\
\textbf{Language} & Python (\texttt{heapq} for the priority queue) \\
\textbf{Output} & Working encoder/decoder + compression analysis \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{1em}

Build a Huffman coder to see Shannon's source coding theorem in action: the minimum average code length equals the entropy.

# The Idea

Frequent characters get short codes, rare characters get long codes. This is optimal for symbol-by-symbol coding.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  level distance=1.2cm,
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
  every node/.style={font=\small, draw, circle, minimum size=6mm},
  edge from parent/.style={draw, ->, thick},
]
  \node {14}
    child { node {6}
      child { node[rectangle] {a:3} edge from parent node[left, draw=none, font=\footnotesize] {0} }
      child { node[rectangle] {b:3} edge from parent node[right, draw=none, font=\footnotesize] {1} }
      edge from parent node[left, draw=none, font=\footnotesize] {0}
    }
    child { node {8}
      child { node[rectangle] {c:3} edge from parent node[left, draw=none, font=\footnotesize] {0} }
      child { node[rectangle] {d:5} edge from parent node[right, draw=none, font=\footnotesize] {1} }
      edge from parent node[right, draw=none, font=\footnotesize] {1}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

Codes: a $\to$ \texttt{00}, b $\to$ \texttt{01}, c $\to$ \texttt{10}, d $\to$ \texttt{11}. More skewed frequencies $\to$ more variable-length codes $\to$ better compression.

# Part 1 --- Build the Coder (\~45min)

## 1a. `build_tree(freq_dict)`

- Start with each character as a leaf node, weighted by frequency
- Use a min-heap (`heapq`): repeatedly pop the two lightest nodes, merge them into a parent with combined weight, push the parent back
- Continue until one node remains --- that's the root

## 1b. `build_codes()`

Walk the tree recursively. Going left appends `'0'`, going right appends `'1'`. At each leaf, record the code.

## 1c. `encode(text)` and `decode(bits)`

- Encode: concatenate each character's code
- Decode: walk the tree bit by bit, emit a character when you reach a leaf, restart from root

# Part 2 --- Test and Verify (\~30min)

## 2a. Basic test

Encode `"hello world"`. Print each character's code. Verify:
$$\texttt{decode(encode("hello world"))} = \texttt{"hello world"}$$

## 2b. Shannon's theorem check

Compute the **entropy** of the character distribution:
$$H = -\sum_c p(c) \log_2 p(c)$$

Compute the **average code length**:
$$\bar{L} = \sum_c p(c) \cdot \text{len}(\text{code}(c))$$

Shannon's source coding theorem guarantees:
$$H \leq \bar{L} < H + 1$$

Print $H$, $\bar{L}$, and verify this inequality holds.

## 2c. Compression on real text

Take a paragraph of English text (100+ characters). Compute:

- Original size: $8 \times \text{len(text)}$ bits (ASCII)
- Compressed size: len(encode(text)) bits
- Compression ratio and comparison to entropy lower bound

# Deliverables

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Working \texttt{HuffmanCoder} with \texttt{build\_tree}, \texttt{encode}, \texttt{decode}
  \item Roundtrip verification passes
  \item Printed entropy vs.\ average code length confirming Shannon's bound
\end{enumerate}
